KeySelector} and it gets\\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\\n90:  *\\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\\n95:  *\\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\\n100:  */\\n101: @Internal\\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\\n105: \\n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\\n107: \\n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n111: \\n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\\n113: \\n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\\n115: \\n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\\n117: \\n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\\n119: \\n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\\n122: \\n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\\n125: \\n126:     /**\\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\\n83:  * Trigger}.\\n84:  *\\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\\n90:  *\\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\\n95:  *\\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\\n100:  */\\n101: @Internal\\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\\n105: \\n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\\n107: \\n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n111: \\n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\\n113: \\n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\\n115: \\n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\\n117: \\n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\\n119: \\n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\\n122: \\n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\\n125: \\n126:     /**\\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. triggerContext.key, window, processContext, contents, timestampedCollector);\\n580:     }\\n581: \\n582:     /**\\n583:      * Write skipped late arriving element to SideOutput.\\n584:      *\\n585: java.util.Collection;\n77: \n78: import static org.apache.flink.util.Preconditions.checkArgument;\n79: import static org.apache.flink.util.Preconditions.checkNotNull;\n80: \n81: /**\n82:  * An operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\n83:  * Trigger}.\n84:  *\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\n90:  *\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\n95:  *\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\n100:  */\n101: @Internal\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\n105: \n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\n107: \n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n111: \n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\n113: \n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\n115: \n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\n117: \n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\n119: \n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\n122: \n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\n125: \n126:     /**\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. This is used for:\n128:      *\n129:      * <ul>\n130:      *   <li>Deciding if an element should be dropped from a window due to lateness.\n131:      *   <li>Clearing the state of a window if the system time passes the {@code window.maxTimestamp\n132:      *       + allowedLateness} landmark.\n133:      * </ul>\n134:      */\n135:     protected final long allowedLateness;\n136: \n137:     /**\n138:      * {@link OutputTag} to use for late arriving events. Elements for which {@code\n139:      * window.maxTimestamp + allowedLateness} is smaller than the current watermark will be emitted\n140:      * to this.\n141:      */\n142:     protected final OutputTag<IN> lateDataOutputTag;\n143: \n144:     private static final String LATE_ELEMENTS_DROPPED_METRIC_NAME = \"numLateRecordsDropped\";\n145: \n146:     protected transient Counter numLateRecordsDropped;\n147: \n148:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n149:     // State that is not checkpointed\n150:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n151: \n152:     /** The state in which the window contents is stored. Each window is a namespace */\n153:     private transient InternalAppendingState<K, W, IN, ACC, ACC> windowState;\n154: \n155:     /**\n156:      * The {@link #windowState}, typed to merging state for merging windows. Null if the window\n157:      * state is not mergeable.\n158:      */\n159:     private transient InternalMergingState<K, W, IN, ACC, ACC> windowMergingState;\n160: \n161:     /** The state that holds the merging window metadata (the sets that describe what is merged). */\n162:     private transient InternalListState<K, VoidNamespace, Tuple2<W, W>> mergingSetsState;\n163: \n164:     /**\n165: protected transient WindowAssigner.WindowAssignerContext windowAssignerContext;\n175: \n176:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n177:     // State that needs to be checkpointed\n178:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n179: \n180:     protected transient InternalTimerService<W> internalTimerService;\n181: \n182:     /** Creates a new {@code WindowOperator} based on the given policies and user functions. */\n183:     public WindowOperator(\n184:             WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner,\n185:             TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer,\n186:             KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector,\n187:             TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer,\n188:             StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor,\n189:             InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W> windowFunction,\n190:             Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger,\n191:             long allowedLateness,\n192:             OutputTag<IN> lateDataOutputTag) {\n193: \n194:         super(windowFunction);\n195: \n196:         checkArgument(allowedLateness >=         typedTuple, new TypeSerializer[] {windowSerializer, windowSerializer});\n270: \n271:             final ListStateDescriptor<Tuple2<W, W>> mergingSetsStateDescriptor =\n272: null;\n290:     }\n291: \n292:     @Override\n293:     public void processElement(StreamRecord<IN> element) throws Exception {\n294:         final Collection<W> elementWindows =\n295:                 windowAssigner.assignWindows(\n296:                         element.getValue(), element.getTimestamp(), windowAssignerContext);\n297: \n298:         // if element is handled by none of assigned elementWindows\n299:         boolean isSkippedElement = true;\n300: \n301:         final K key = this.<K>getKeyedStateBackend().getCurrentKey();\n302: \n303:         if (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\n304:             MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\n305: \n306:             for (W window : elementWindows) {\n307: \n308:                 // adding }\n349: \n350:                                         triggerContext.key = key;\n351:                                         triggerContext.window = mergeResult;\n352: \n353: triggerContext.window = m;\n357:                                             triggerContext.clear();\n358:                                             deleteCleanupTimer(m);\n359:                                         }\n360: \n361:                                         // merge the merged state windows into the newly resulting\n362:                                         // state window\n363: false;\n412: \n413:                 windowState.setCurrentNamespace(window);\n414:                 windowState.add(element.getValue());\n415: \n416:                 triggerContext.key = key;\n417:      }\n426:                 }\n427: \n428:                 if (triggerResult.isPurge()) {\n429:                     windowState.clear();\n430:                 }\n431:                 registerCleanupTimer(window);\n432:             }\n433:         }\n434: \n435:         // side output input event if\n436:         // element not handled by any window\n437:         // late arriving tag has been set\n438:         // windowAssigner is event time and current timestamp + allowed lateness no less than\n439:         // element timestamp\n440:         if (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element)) {\n441:             if (lateDataOutputTag != null) {\n442:                 sideOutput(element);\n443:             } else {\n444:                 this.numLateRecordsDropped.inc();\n445:             }\n446:         }\n447:     }\n448: \n449:     @Override\n450:     public void onEventTime(InternalTimer<K, W> timer) throws Exception {\n451:         triggerContext.key = timer.getKey();\n452:         triggerContext.window = timer.getNamespace();\n453: \n454:         MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows;\n455: \n456:         if (windowAssigner We have already cleared the merging\n462:                 // window and therefore the Trigger state, however, so nothing to do.\n463:                 return;\n464:             } else {\n465: @Override\n497:     public void onProcessingTime(InternalTimer<K, W> timer) throws Exception {\n498:         triggerContext.key = timer.getKey();\n499:         triggerContext.window = timer.getNamespace();\n500: \n501:         MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows;\n502: \n503:         if (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\n504:             mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\n505:             W stateWindow = mergingWindows.getStateWindow(triggerContext.window);\n506:             if (stateWindow == null) {\n507:                 // Timer firing for non-existent window, this can only happen if a\n508:                 // trigger did not clean up timers. We have already cleared the merging\n509:                 // window and therefore the Trigger state, however, so nothing to do.\n510:                 return;\n511:             } else {\n512:                 windowState.setCurrentNamespace(stateWindow);\n513:             }\n514:         } else {\n515:             windowState.setCurrentNamespace(triggerContext.window);\n516:             mergingWindows = null;\n517:         }\n518: \n519:         TriggerResult triggerResult = triggerContext.onProcessingTime(timer.getTimestamp());\n520: \n521:         if (triggerResult.isFire()) {\n522:             ACC contents = windowState.get();\n523:             if (contents != null) {\n524:                 emitWindowContents(triggerContext.window, contents);\n525:             }\n526:         }\n527: \n528:         if (triggerResult.isPurge()) {\n529:             windowState.clear();\n530:         }\n531: \n532:         if (!windowAssigner.isEventTime()\n533:                 && @Override\n544:     public OperatorAttributes getOperatorAttributes() {\n545:         boolean isOutputOnlyAfterEndOfStream =\n546:                 windowAssigner instanceof GlobalWindows\n547:                         && trigger instanceof GlobalWindows.EndOfStreamTrigger;\n548:         return new OperatorAttributesBuilder()\n549:                 .setOutputOnlyAfterEndOfStream(isOutputOnlyAfterEndOfStream)\n550:                 .build();\n551:     }\n552: \n553:     /**\n554:      * Drops all state for the given window and calls {@link Trigger#clear(Window,\n555:      * Trigger.TriggerContext)}.\n556:      *\n557:      * <p>The caller must ensure that the correct key is set in the state backend and the\n558:      * triggerContext object.\n559:      */\n560:     private void clearAllState(\n561:             W window, AppendingState<IN, ACC> windowState, MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows)\n562:             throws Exception {\n563:         windowState.clear();\n564:         triggerContext.clear();\n565:         processContext.window = window;\n566:         processContext.clear();\n567:         if (mergingWindows != null) {@link InternalWindowFunction}. */\n574:     @SuppressWarnings(\"unchecked\")\n575:     private void emitWindowContents(W window, ACC contents) throws Exception {\n576: arriving element to side output\n586:      */\n587:     protected void sideOutput(StreamRecord<IN> element) {\n588:         output.collect(lateDataOutputTag, element);\n589:     }\n590: \n591:     /**\n592:      * Retrieves the {@link MergingWindowSet} for the currently active key. The caller must ensure\n593:      * that the correct key is set in the state backend.\n594:      *\n595:      * <p>The caller must also ensure to properly persist changes to state using {@link\n596:      * MergingWindowSet#persist()}.\n597:      */\n598:     protected MergingWindowSet<W> getMergingWindowSet() throws Exception {\n599:         @SuppressWarnings(\"unchecked\")\n600: */\n609:     protected boolean isWindowLate(W window) {\n610:         return (windowAssigner.isEventTime()\n611:                 && (cleanupTime(window) <= internalTimerService.currentWatermark()));\n612:     }\n613: \n614:     /**\n615:      * Decide if a record is currently late, based on current watermark and allowed lateness.\n616:      *\n617:      * @param element The element to check\n618:      * @return The element for which should be considered when sideoutputs\n619:      */\n620:     protected boolean isElementLate(StreamRecord<IN> element) {\n621:         return (windowAssigner.isEventTime())\n622:                 && (element.getTimestamp() + allowedLateness\n623:                         <= internalTimerService.currentWatermark());\n624:     }\n625: \n626:     /**\n627:      * Registers a timer to cleanup the content of the window.\n628:      *\n629:      * @param window the window whose state to discard\n630:      */\n631:     protected void registerCleanupTimer(W window) {\n632:         long cleanupTime = cleanupTime(window);\n633:         if (cleanupTime == Long.MAX_VALUE) {\n634:             // don't set a GC timer for \"end of time\"\n635:             return;\n636:         }\n637: \n638:         if (windowAssigner.isEventTime()) {\n639:             triggerContext.registerEventTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\n640:         } else {\n641:             triggerContext.registerProcessingTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\n642:         }\n643:     }\n644: \n645:     /**\n646:      * Deletes the cleanup timer set for the contents of the provided window.\n647:      *\n648:      * @param window the window whose state to discard\n649:      */\n650:     protected void deleteCleanupTimer(W window) {\n651:         long cleanupTime = cleanupTime(window);\n652:         if (cleanupTime == Long.MAX_VALUE) {\n653:             // no need to clean up because we didn't set one\n654:             return;\n655:         }\n656:         if (windowAssigner.isEventTime()) {\n657:             triggerContext.deleteEventTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\n658:         } else {\n659:             triggerContext.deleteProcessingTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\n660:         }\n661:     }\n662: \n663:     /**\n664:      * Returns the cleanup time for a window, which is {@code window.maxTimestamp +\n665:      * allowedLateness}. In case this leads to a value greater than {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} then a\n666:      * cleanup time of {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} is returned.\n667:      *\n668:      * @param window the window whose cleanup time we are computing.\n669:      */\n670:     private long cleanupTime(W window) {\n671:         if (windowAssigner.isEventTime()) {\n672:             long cleanupTime = window.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness;\n673:             return cleanupTime >= window.maxTimestamp() ? cleanupTime : Long.MAX_VALUE;\n674:         } else {\n675:             return window.maxTimestamp();\n676:         }\n677:     }\n678: \n679:     /** Returns {@code true} if the given time is the cleanup time for the given window. */\n680:     protected final boolean isCleanupTime(W window, long time) {\n681:         return time == cleanupTime(window);\n682:     }\n683: \n684:     /**\n685:      * Base class for per-window {@link KeyedStateStore KeyedStateStores}. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01ANrvvXxLzjx2GGFFfy2rWF","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Perfect! Now I can see the issue. Let me analyze the `processElement` method (lines 293-447):\n\nLooking at the code:\n\nFor merging windows (lines 303-403):\n```java\nif (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\n    MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\n\n    for (W window : elementWindows) {\n        // Add to window set - this might trigger merge\n        W actualWindow = mergingWindows.addWindow(window, new MergingWindowSet.MergeFunction<W>() {\n            // ... merge logic ...\n        });\n\n        // drop if the window is already late (LINE 369)\n        if (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n            mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n            continue;\n        }\n        isSkippedElement = false;\n        // ... process element ...\n    }\n    mergingWindows.persist();\n}\n```\n\nThen at the end (lines 440-446):\n```java\n// side output input event if\n// element not handled by any window\n// late arriving tag has been set\n// windowAssigner is event time and current timestamp + allowed lateness no less than\n// element timestamp\nif (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element)) {\n    if (lateDataOutputTag != null) {\n        sideOutput(element);\n    } else {\n        this.numLateRecordsDropped.inc();\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThe problem is:\n1. For merging windows, when an element arrives, it's first checked with `isWindowLate(actualWindow)` at line 369\n2. If it's late, the element is skipped (continue at line 371) and `isSkippedElement` remains true\n3. Later, at line 440, `isElementLate(element)` is checked to determine if we should side output\n\n\n4. The issue is that `isWindowLate()` evaluates the merged window's cleanup time against the watermark, but an element could still be mergeable into an existing session even if that check fails. For session windows specifically, the element might arrive after the watermark yet still extend an active session, so rejecting it based on cleanup time is premature.\n\nWhen an element with a timestamp past the watermark reaches `processElement()`, the code marks it as late. However, for merging windows, this element could extend an existing session that hasn't been cleaned up. The late element handling then attempts to side-output it, but if no late data tag is configured, the element gets silently dropped instead of being merged into the session. The real problem is that we're checking if the window is late before actually merging. For session windows, an element arriving after the watermark might still extend an existing window, but we reject it as late too early. Then when we try to emit it to the side output, the tag isn't registered with the collector, causing the failure. a late merging window case would be: an element arrives after the watermark but within `allowedLateness` of an existing session, gets assigned to window W, and when W is merged with existing windows to form W_merged, the merged window is checked with `isWindowLate(W_merged)`. If the merged window's max timestamp plus allowed lateness is at or before the current watermark, it's considered late and skipped, leaving `isSkippedElement` as true. Then at the final check, since both `isSkippedElement` and `isElementLate(element)` are true, the element should be side-output if `lateDataOutputTag` is configured, otherwise dropped.\n\nBut the tag might be null somewhere in the execution path, or perhaps the issue lies in how `EvictingWindowOperator` handles this scenario differently.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":11,"cache_creation_input_tokens":13686,"cache_read_input_tokens":42092,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":13686},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c978f4f8-d7a7-4f06-b0d6-88f2c361e8bc","uuid":"e3255ea5-0fe4-4675-bd4d-76074773ea7d"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01KJH85Wns8qt5ezjJa1Rogj","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now I understand the structure better. Looking at the issue more carefully:\n\nThe problem is in the order of operations for merging windows in WindowOperator.processElement():\n\n1. For merging windows, when an element arrives:\n   - We call `mergingWindows.addWindow(window, mergeFunction)` \n   - This might merge the new window with existing windows\n   - We get `actualWindow` which is the result (the merged window or the same window if no merge)\n\n2. Then at line 369:\n   ```java\n   if (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n       mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n       continue;\n   }\n   ```\n   - We check if the merged window is late\n   - `isWindowLate()` checks if `cleanupTime(window) <= currentWatermark()`\n   - `cleanupTime(window)` = `window.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness`\n\n3. The issue: \n   - An element arrives with timestamp T after the watermark\n   - The element is assigned to window W\n   - W is merged with existing window W_old to form W_merged\n   - If W_merged.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness <= currentWatermark, we consider it late and skip it\n   - But the element could have validly extended the session window!\n\n4. Then at the end:\n   ```java\n   if (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element)) {\n       if (lateDataOutputTag != null) {\n           sideOutput(element);\n       }\n   }\n   ```\n   - Since the element was skipped, it might be side-outputted as late\n\nBut the real issue is: the element shouldn't have been skipped in the first place if it could have extended an existing window!\n\nI think the fix is: We need to check if the element would have been merged into an existing window before we mark it as late. If the merge happened and the element would be added to state, then we shouldn't treat it as completely late.\n\n\nActually, re-reading the bug description: \"the side output emission is skipped because the output tag is null at that point in the code path\". Let me reconsider what's happening. When we retire the window at line 369 and set `isSkippedElement = true`, we later attempt to side-output the element if it's late. But the `lateDataOutputTag` should be available since it's initialized in the constructor, so that's not the issue. The real problem seems to be that we're retiring the merged window too early—before we've had a chance to process the element that could extend it.\n\nI should write a test to verify this behavior. The scenario is: a session window with `allowedLateness(0)` receives an element after the watermark passes. This element could merge with an existing session, but instead of being side-outputted, it gets silently dropped. Let me implement a fix based on this understanding.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":10,"cache_creation_input_tokens":3294,"cache_read_input_tokens":77879,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":3294},"output_tokens":3,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c978f4f8-d7a7-4f06-b0d6-88f2c361e8bc","uuid":"447c23ad-5a9f-4d4d-ac90-89f0757e11ca"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_014DjJmAStWxuown61myVH7j","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Perfect! Now I see the test pattern. The comment at line 2842-2851 is very revealing:\n\n```\n// because of the small allowed lateness and because the trigger is accumulating\n// this will be merged into the session (11600-14600) and therefore will not\n// be sideoutput as late\ntestHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\\\"key2\\\", 1), 10000));\ntestHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\\\"key2\\\", 1), 14500));\n// adding (\"key2\", 1) extended the session to (10000-146000) for which\n// maxTimestamp <= currentWatermark. Therefore, we immediately get a firing\n// with the current version of EventTimeTrigger/EventTimeTriggerAccum\n```\n\nThis shows the current behavior: when an element arrives late and gets merged into an existing window, it's supposed to be processed normally (not side-outputted).\n\nBut the bug description suggests there's a scenario where:\n1. An element arrives that could be merged into an existing window\n2. But the merged window's cleanup time is <= currentWatermark\n3. So the element is dropped without being side-outputted\n\nLet me create a specific test case that reproduces this bug. The key difference from the existing test is:\n- We have allowedLateness = 0 (zero lateness)\n- An element arrives after the watermark but could extend an existing session that hasn't been cleaned up yet\n- The merged window's maxTimestamp + allowedLateness <= currentWatermark, so isWindowLate() returns true\n- The element should be side-outputted as late, but it's being silently dropped\n\nLet me now understand the actual bug by reading the code more carefully. Looking at WindowOperator.java lines 303-403:\n\nFor merging windows:\n```java\nif (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\n    MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\n    for (W window : elementWindows) {\n        W actualWindow = mergingWindows.addWindow(window, new MergingWindowSet.MergeFunction<W>() {\n            // ... merge logic ...\n        });\n        \n        // drop if the window is already late (LINE 369)\n        if (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n            mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n            continue;  // <-- PROBLEM: we skip processing, and isSkippedElement remains true\n        }\n        isSkippedElement = false\n```\n\nThe issue is clear now: when a merged window is determined to be late, the code continues without setting `isSkippedElement = false`. This means the element gets silently dropped instead of being side-outputted as late data.\n\nThe root cause is that `isSkippedElement` starts as true and only gets set to false when an element successfully processes through a window. But if every assigned window is late, the flag never changes, so the late-data handling logic at the end never executes—the element is lost entirely rather than being captured in the late-data side output. W actualWindow returned by addWindow is the result after merging. If its cleanup time falls at or before the current watermark, it's treated as late. However, there's a scenario where this causes issues: when element E1 arrives before the watermark and creates window W1[T1, T1+gap], then E2 arrives after the watermark with timestamp T2 where T2 < T1+gap, causing it to merge with W1. The merged window's cleanup time might then be evaluated as late even though the merge itself should have been handled differently.\n\nThe real problem emerges in how late elements are handled when windows are skipped. If all windows assigned to an element end up being skipped due to lateness, the element gets marked as skipped. Then when checking if it's late, if a late data output tag exists, it gets side-output, but if the tag is null, it just increments a counter without actually processing the element.\n\nFor merging windows specifically, there's a deeper issue: an element arriving after the watermark might still be able to extend an existing session window that hasn't been cleaned up yet. The current logic doesn't account for this possibility—it treats the element as purely late without considering whether it could legitimately merge with an active window.\n\nThe contradiction here is that if a window's cleanup time has already passed the watermark, the window should be closed and no new elements should be accepted into it. Yet the problem statement suggests the element should have been merged. This points to either a flaw in how we determine window lateness for merging scenarios, or a misunderstanding of when elements should actually be rejected versus merged.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":10,"cache_creation_input_tokens":2094,"cache_read_input_tokens":84125,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":2094},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c978f4f8-d7a7-4f06-b0d6-88f2c361e8bc","uuid":"e84c863c-c62b-4657-a0b1-2dc0107256bb"}

Now I have all the information I need. Let me now understand the exact bug and create a test case for it. Based on my analysis of the code:\n\nThe issue is:\n1. For merging windows (session windows), when an element arrives that could extend an existing session window, the code calls `mergingWindows.addWindow()` which may merge it with an existing window.\n2. Then it checks `isWindowLate(actualWindow)` - if the merged window's cleanup time <= currentWatermark, it's considered late and skipped with `continue`.\n3. This causes `isSkippedElement` to remain true.\n4. Later, at the end of `processElement()`, it checks `if (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element))` and tries to side-output the element.\n5. But the problem is: the element SHOULD have been processed as part of the merged window, not side-outputted as late. However, if the merged window is late, the element gets dropped without even trying to side-output it properly.\n\nActually, wait. Looking at the code again at line 369:\n```java\nif (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n    mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n    continue;\n}\nisSkippedElement = false;\n```\n\nIf we reach the `continue` statement, `isSkippedElement` is not set to false. So when we later check `if (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element))`, it will be true and we'll try to side-output.\n\nSo the code should be working correctly! But the bug description suggests the side-output is being skipped. Let me re-read the bug description once more...\n\n\"The bug occurs because the merging window operator's `processElement()` method checks lateness BEFORE merging, so an element that arrives after the watermark but could extend a session window is incorrectly classified as late\"\n\nAh! The problem is \"checks lateness BEFORE merging\". \n\nBut examining the actual code flow, the merge operation happens first at line 312, followed by the lateness check at line 369. The bug description might be describing an earlier version or different code path. Looking more carefully at lines 303-366, I see the `MergingWindowSet<W>` is obtained and for each window, `mergingWindows.addWindow()` is called which performs the merge operation through the provided `MergeFunction`. This is where windows get combined before any lateness evaluation occurs.\n\nThe sequence is: assign windows via `windowAssigner.assignWindows()`, then for each assigned window, call `mergingWindows.addWindow()` to merge if applicable and get the `actualWindow` result, then check `isWindowLate(actualWindow)` and skip processing if true. The issue might be that when a merge produces a new window, that merged window could be late even though the original assigned windows weren't, causing the element to be dropped when it should have been processed.\n\nLooking at the merge function's validation logic, it explicitly checks whether a merged window would exceed the allowed lateness threshold relative to the current watermark. If the merge would create a late window, an exception gets thrown. This suggests the framework is designed to prevent merging into late windows, but the question becomes whether this exception handling is the actual problem or if there's a different scenario where elements are incorrectly discarded.\n\nThe sequence appears to be: an element with timestamp T arrives after the watermark, gets assigned to window W, then merges with an existing window W_old to form W_merged. At that point, if W_merged.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness <= currentWatermark evaluates to true, the merge operation fails. So the merge only succeeds when the resulting window isn't late—meaning mergeResult.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness > currentWatermark must hold.\n\nAfter a successful merge, the code checks isWindowLate(actualWindow) using cleanupTime(window) <= currentWatermark, where cleanupTime equals window.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness. This is the exact same condition the merge function already validated. If the merge succeeded, isWindowLate should return false, suggesting a logical inconsistency unless actualWindow differs from the merged result or the merge function wasn't invoked at all.\n\nWhen no merge occurs (actualWindow == window), the merge function never runs, so isWindowLate becomes the only check. If it returns true, the element gets marked as skipped and should be side-output if lateDataOutputTag is configured. The logic appears sound, but since the bug persists, either lateDataOutputTag is null, or there's something else preventing the side-output from happening.\n\nI should write a test to isolate the issue—one that uses session windows with allowedLateness(0), configures a side output tag, sends a late element past the watermark, and verifies whether it actually appears in the side output. This will help pinpoint whether the problem is in the configuration, the sideOutput call itself, or somewhere in how the output is being collected.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":9,"cache_creation_input_tokens":954,"cache_read_input_tokens":101171,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":954},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c978f4f8-d7a7-4f06-b0d6-88f2c361e8bc","uuid":"2fa60f30-ed0b-47e3-b346-183d7edf9e30"}

Integer>>(\"late-output\") {};\n\n    /**\n     * Test case: Late element that could extend a session window should be side-outputted\n     *\n     * Scenario:\n     * 1. Element E1 arrives at T=1000, creates session window [1000, 4000]\n     * 2. Watermark advances to T=3999\n     * 3. Element E2 arrives at T=3800 (within session gap), would extend to [1000, 6800]\n     * 4. Watermark advances to T=4001\n     * 5. Element E3 arrives at T=3500 (after watermark, could extend [1000, 6300])\n     *    - isElementLate(E3) returns true because T=3500 + allowedLateness=0 <= 4001\n     *    - But E3 could extend the window, so it should be processed, not side-outputted\n     *\n     * Expected: E3 should be side-outputted as late since the window is already closed\n     */\n    @Test\n    void testLateDataWithSessionWindowZeroLateness() throws Exception {\n        final int gapSize = 3; // 3000 ms gap\n        final long allowedLateness = 0; // No allowed lateness\n\n                       STRING_INT_TUPLE.createSerializer(new SerializerConfigImpl()));\n\n        WindowOperatorFactory<\n                String,\n                Tuple2<String, Integer>,\n session window [1000, 4000]\n        testHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 1000));\n\n        // Advance watermark to 3999 (before window closes)\n        testHarness.processWatermark(new Watermark(3999));\n        expected.add(new Watermark(3999));\n\n        // Element 2: Arrive at T=3800, extends window to [1000, 6800]\n        testHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 3800));\n\n        // Advance watermark to 4001 (window [1000, 4000] is now closed)\n        testHarness.processWatermark(new Watermark(4001));\n        expected.add(new Watermark(4001));\n\n        // Element 3: Arrive at T=3500, after watermark, could extend [1000, 6300]\n        // This element is late (3500 + 0 <= 4001) but should be side-outputted\n        testHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 3500));\n        sideOutputExpected.add(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 3500));\n\n        // Continue processing\n        testHarness.processWatermark(new Watermark(10000));\n        expected.add(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple3<>(\"key1-3\", 1000L, 6800L), 6799));\n        expected.add(new Watermark(10000));\n\n        // Verify main output\n        ConcurrentLinkedQueue<Object> actual = testHarness.getOutput();\n        TestHarnessUtil.assertOutputEqualsSorted(\n private static class SumReducer implements org.apache.flink.api.common.functions.ReduceFunction<Tuple2<String, Integer>> {\n        @Override\n        public Tuple2<String, Integer> reduce(Tuple2<String, Integer> value1, Tuple2<String, Integer> value2) throws Exception {\n            return new Tuple2<>(value1.f0, value1.f1 + value2.f1);\n        }\n    }\n\n    private static org.apache.flink.api.common.typeinfo.TypeHint<Tuple2<String, Integer>>() {});\n\n    private static final OutputTag<Tuple2<String, Integer>> lateOutputTag =\n            new OutputTag<Tuple2<String, Integer>>(\"late-output\") {};\n\n    /**\n     * Test case: Late element that could extend a session window should be side-outputted\n     *\n     * Scenario:\n     * 1. Element E1 arrives at T=1000, creates session window [1000, 4000]\n     * 2. Watermark advances to T=3999\n     * 3. Element E2 arrives at T=3800 (within session gap), would extend to [1000, 6800]\n     * 4. Watermark advances to T=4001\n     * 5. Element E3 arrives at T=3500 (after watermark, could extend [1000, 6300])\n     *    - isElementLate(E3) returns true because T=3500 + allowedLateness=0 <= 4001\n     *    - But E3 could extend the window, so it should be processed, not side-outputted\n     *\n     * Expected: E3 should be side-outputted as late since the window is already closed\n     */\n    @Test\n    void testLateDataWithSessionWindowZeroLateness() throws Exception {\n        final int gapSize = 3; // 3000 ms gap\n        final long allowedLateness = 0; // No allowed lateness\n\n        ReducingStateDescriptor<Tuple2<String, Integer>> stateDesc =\n                new ReducingStateDescriptor<>(\n                        \"window-contents\",\n                        new SumReducer(),\n                        STRING_INT_TUPLE.createSerializer(new SerializerConfigImpl()));\n\n        WindowOperatorFactory<\n                String,\n                Tuple2<String, Integer>,\n                Tuple2<String, Integer>,\n                Tuple3<String, Long, Long>,\n                TimeWindow>\n        operator =\n                new WindowOperatorFactory<>(\n                        EventTimeSessionWindows.withGap(Duration.ofSeconds(gapSize)),\n                        new TimeWindow.Serializer(),\n                        new TupleKeySelector(),\n                        BasicTypeInfo.STRING_TYPE_INFO.createSerializer(new SerializerConfigImpl()),\n                        stateDesc,\n                        new InternalSingleValueWindowFunction<>(new ReducedSessionWindowFunction()),\n                        EventTimeTrigger.create(),\n                        allowedLateness,\n                        lateOutputTag);\n\n        OneInputStreamOperatorTestHarness<Tuple2<String, Integer>, Tuple3<String, Long, Long>>\n                testHarness = createTestHarness(operator);\n\n        testHarness.open();\n\n        ConcurrentLinkedQueue<Object> expected = new ConcurrentLinkedQueue<>();\n        ConcurrentLinkedQueue<Object> sideOutputExpected = new ConcurrentLinkedQueue<>();\n\n        // Element 1: Create session window [1000, 4000]\n        testHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 1000));\n\n        // Advance watermark to 3999 (before window closes)\n        testHarness.processWatermark(new Watermark(3999));\n        expected.add(new Watermark(3999));\n\n        // Element 2: Arrive at T=3800, extends window to [1000, 6800]\n        testHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 3800));\n\n        // Advance watermark to 4001 (window [1000, 4000] is now closed)\n        testHarness.processWatermark(new Watermark(4001));\n        expected.add(new Watermark(4001));\n\n        // Element 3: Arrive at T=3500, after watermark, could extend [1000, 6300]\n        // This element is late (3500 + 0 <= 4001) but should be side-outputted\n        testHarness.processElement(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 3500));\n        sideOutputExpected.add(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple2<>(\"key1\", 1), 3500));\n\n        // Continue processing\n        testHarness.processWatermark(new Watermark(10000));\n        expected.add(new StreamRecord<>(new Tuple3<>(\"key1-3\", 1000L, 6800L), 6799));\n        expected.add(new Watermark(10000));\n\n        // Verify main output\n        ConcurrentLinkedQueue<Object> actual = testHarness.getOutput();\n        TestHarnessUtil.assertOutputEqualsSorted(\n                \"Output was not correct.\", expected, actual, new Tuple2ResultSortComparator());\n\n        // Verify side output - THIS IS THE KEY TEST\n        ConcurrentLinkedQueue<StreamRecord<Tuple2<String, Integer>>> sideActual =\n                testHarness.getSideOutput(lateOutputTag);\n\n        assertThat(sideActual).isNotNull().as(\"Side output should not be null\");\n        assertThat(sideActual).isNotEmpty().as(\"Late element should be in side output\");\n\n        testHarness.close();\n    }\n\n    // Helper classes and methods...\n\n    private static class SumReducer implements org.apache.flink.api.common.functions.ReduceFunction<Tuple2<String, Integer>> {\n        @Override\n        public Tuple2<String, Integer> reduce(Tuple2<String, Integer> value1, Tuple2<String, Integer> value2) throws Exception {\n            return new Tuple2<>(value1.f0, value1.f1 + value2.f1);\n        }\n    }\n\n    private static class ReducedSessionWindowFunction extends org.apache.flink.streaming.api.functions.windowing.WindowFunction<Tuple2<String, Integer>, Tuple3<String, Long, Long>, String, TimeWindow> {\n        @Override\n        public void apply(String key, TimeWindow window, Iterable<Tuple2<String, Integer>> input, org.apache.flink.util.Collector<Tuple3<String, Long, Long>> out) throws Exception {\n            int count = 0;\n            for (Tuple2<String, Integer> item : input) {\n                count += item.f1;\n            }\n            out.collect(new Tuple3<>(key + \"-\" + count, window.getStart(), window.getEnd()));\n        }\n    }\n\n    private static class TupleKeySelector implements org.apache.flink.api.java.functions.KeySelector<Tuple2<String, Integer>, String> {\n        @Override\n        public String getKey(Tuple2<String, Integer> value) throws Exception {\n            return value.f0;\n        }\n    }\n\n    private static <OUT> OneInputStreamOperatorTestHarness<Tuple2<String, Integer>, OUT> createTestHarness(\n            OneInputStreamOperatorFactory<Tuple2<String, Integer>, OUT> operator) {\n        try {\n RuntimeException(e);\n        }\n    }\n\n    private static class Tuple2ResultSortComparator implements java.util.Comparator<Object> {\n        @Override\n        public int compare(Object o1, Object This sets `isSkippedElement = true` for that window, but if the element is assigned to multiple windows and one of them is not late, we'll process it normally and set `isSkippedElement = false`.\n\nHowever, the real issue might be that for merging windows specifically, when an element is late but could have extended an existing window, we should still try to process it or at least side-output it properly.\n\nLet me re-examine the bug description once more: \"the merging window operator's `processElement()` method checks lateness BEFORE merging\"\n\nThis suggests the current code (which I've read) checks lateness AFTER merging (at line 369). This prevents the merge from happening.\n\nBut then after the merge (lines 369-371):\n```java\nif (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n    mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n    continue;\n}\n```\n\nThis checks if the actualWindow is late. But if the merge function already threw an exception for a late result, how can actualWindow be late here?\n\nUnless... what if the element gets assigned to a new window W that doesn't merge with anything? Then:\n\n\n- No merge function call\n- actualWindow == W\n- But W might be late (because the merge check is skipped)\n- So we skip W\n\nThis should work correctly based on the late data side-output logic at the end. The real issue might be more subtle. When a window that isn't merged could still be late: an element E arrives with timestamp T, gets assigned to window W by the window assigner, and mergingWindows.addWindow(W) is called to try merging W with existing windows. During the merge function call, the mergeResult is checked for lateness, but if the merge result would be late, an exception is thrown and the merge doesn't happen.\n\nSo the element gets assigned to W, and addWindow returns W without merging. Then isWindowLate(W) is checked—W might be late if it was newly created and its maxTimestamp + allowedLateness <= currentWatermark, so it gets skipped. This seems correct; the element should be side-outputted as late. The merge check does throw an exception if a merge would result in a late window, so the merge fails. But there could be a scenario where a new element arrives and could merge with multiple existing windows, though the merge check fails for some reason, leaving the element in a new unmerged window W that's late and gets skipped. In that case, the code should side-output it. Looking at the late data side-output logic, it checks if the element was skipped and is late, then side-outputs it if a lateDataOutputTag is set, otherwise increments numLateRecordsDropped.\n\nThe condition `isSkippedElement` tracks whether the element made it into any window—it's set to false when successfully added (line 373), so if it remains true, the element wasn't assigned anywhere and was dropped. The side-output happens when an element fails to be handled by any window and arrives late.\n\nLooking at the `sideOutput` method, it simply calls `output.collect()` with the registered tag, which appears correct. Re-examining the bug description more carefully: the issue suggests that when using merging windows with `allowedLateness(0)`, an element could theoretically be merged into an existing window that hasn't been cleaned up, but the merge check prevents this if the result would be late. The real problem might be that the `lateDataOutputTag` isn't properly registered with the output collector during operator initialization.\n\nFor a concrete scenario: when element E arrives after the watermark but within allowed lateness, it gets assigned to window W. If W merges with an existing window to form W_merged, and that merged window isn't late, the merge succeeds. But if the merged window is late, the element gets skipped and should be side-output. The code appears to handle this, unless there's an issue with how `isElementLate` or `isWindowLate` are implemented, or how the side output is actually collected.\n\nLooking at the merge logic itself: the merge function throws an exception if the result would be late, which causes the entire `processElement()` call to fail rather than gracefully handling the late element. This might be where the element gets silently dropped instead of being side-output.\n\nLet me reconsider the flow: when `addWindow()` is called, it only invokes the merge function if there are actually windows to merge with. If a new window has no merge candidates, the function never executes and no exception is thrown. The element then proceeds through normal late-window checks. But if merging does occur and the check fails, the exception propagates up and the element isn't processed or side-outputted at all—it's just lost. method doesn't have exception handling around the merge call. If the merge function throws when attempting to combine an element into a late window, the entire processElement() fails rather than gracefully handling the late data.\n\nThe core issue is that an exception during merging propagates up and breaks the pipeline instead of side-outputting the element. I could catch the exception and side-output as late, check before merging whether it would create a late window, or skip merges that would be problematic. The cleanest approach seems to be validating whether a merge would result in lateness before attempting it, rather than throwing an exception after the fact.\n\nHowever, the merge logic is embedded within addWindow and called during the merge process itself, so I can't easily pre-check the outcome. Since I don't have full visibility into the window assigner implementation, the most practical solution is to wrap the merge operation in a try-catch block. KeySelector} and it gets\\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\\n90:  *\\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\\n95:  *\\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\\n100:  */\\n101: @Internal\\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\\n105: \\n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\\n107: \\n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n111: \\n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\\n113: \\n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\\n115: \\n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\\n117: \\n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\\n119: \\n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\\n122: \\n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\\n125: \\n126:     /**\\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. This is used for:\\n128:      *\\n129:      * <ul>\\n130:      *   <li>Deciding if an element should be dropped from a window due to lateness.\\n131:      *   <li>Clearing the state of a window if the system time passes the {@code window.maxTimestamp\\n132:      *       + allowedLateness} landmark.\\n133:      * </ul>\\n134:      */\\n135:     protected final long allowedLateness;\\n136: \\n137:     /**\\n138:      * {@link OutputTag} to use for late arriving events. Elements for which {@code\\n139:      * window.maxTimestamp + allowedLateness} is smaller than the current watermark will be emitted\\n140:      * to this.\\n141:      */\\n142:     protected final OutputTag<IN> lateDataOutputTag;\\n143: \\n144:     private static final String LATE_ELEMENTS_DROPPED_METRIC_NAME = \\\"numLateRecordsDropped\\\";\\n145: \\n146:     protected transient Counter numLateRecordsDropped;\\n147: \\n148:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n149:     // State that is not checkpointed\\n150:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n151: \\n152:     /** The state in which the window contents is stored. operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\\n83:  * Trigger}.\\n84:  *\\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\\n90:  *\\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\\n95:  *\\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\\n100:  */\\n101: @Internal\\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\\n105: \\n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\\n107: \\n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n111: \\n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\\n113: \\n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\\n115: \\n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\\n117: \\n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\\n119: \\n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\\n122: \\n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\\n125: \\n126:     /**\\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\n83:  * Trigger}.\n84:  *\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\n90:  *\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\n95:  *\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\n100:  */\n101: @Internal\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\n105: \n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\n107: \n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n111: \n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\n113: \n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\n115: \n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\n117: \n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\n119: \n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\n122: \n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\n125: \n126:     /**\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_014QsWDfovPSxtziPWyTJJ4y","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now I realize the actual bug based on my careful reading of the code and the problem description. The issue is:\n\nWhen an element arrives that could be merged into an existing window, but the merge would result in a late window, the merge function throws an UnsupportedOperationException (lines 327-334). This exception is NOT caught in the processElement() method, so it propagates up and causes the entire processElement() call to fail.\n\nThe fix is to catch this exception and treat the element as late, side-outputting it if a late data output tag is configured.\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-read the description one more time: \"the side output emission is skipped because the output tag is null at that point in the code path\"This suggests the output tag is NULL, not that an exception is thrown. So maybe the issue is different.\n\nActually, looking at the code more carefully, I think I finally understand the real issue:\n\nThe problem is that for merging windows, when we merge an element into an existing window, the merge function checks if the merge result would be late (lines 323-334). If it would be late, it throws an exception.\n\nBut here's the key: this exception is thrown INSIDE the merge function, which is a callback. The addWindow method calls this merge function during the merge process.\n\nIf the merge function throws an exception, what happens? Does the addWindow method handle it? Or does it propagate?\n\nLooking at the code, I don't see any try-catch around the addWindow call in processElement(). So if the merge function throws an exception, it would propagate up and cause processElement() to fail.\n\nThis is BAD because the element is lost and not even side-outputted as late!\n\nThe fix is to:1. Catch any exceptions thrown during the merge process\n2. If an exception is caught, treat the element as late and side-output it\n\nOr alternatively:1. Move the lateness check BEFORE the merge attempt\n2. If the merge would result in a late window, skip the merge and mark the element as late\n\n\nI'll go with wrapping the merge operation in a try-catch block to handle the exception gracefully and ensure late elements get properly side-outputted.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":9,"cache_creation_input_tokens":7368,"cache_read_input_tokens":110022,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":7368},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c978f4f8-d7a7-4f06-b0d6-88f2c361e8bc","uuid":"6469433e-cf54-42de-bd56-5282f3d67073"}

Processing the element normally\n2. Side-outputting it as late data\n3. Incrementing the late records counter\n\n## Solution\n\nAdd exception handling in the merging window loop to catch `UnsupportedOperationException` thrown during merge attempts. When such an exception is caught:\n1. Mark that this window assignment failed\n2. Continue to process other assigned windows\n3. At the end of `processElement()`, if all windows failed and the element is late, side-output it\n\n## Changes Required\n\n### File: flink-runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/flink/streaming/runtime/operators/windowing/WindowOperator.java\n\n**Location**: Lines 303-403 (merging windows processElement path)\n\n**Change**: Wrap the `mergingWindows.addWindow()` call and subsequent window processing in a try-catch block that:\n- Catches `UnsupportedOperationException`\n- Continues to the next window instead of crashing\n- Leaves `isSkippedElement = true` for that window assignment\n- Allows the late data side-output logic at the end to handle the element\n\n### File: flink-runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/flink/streaming/runtime/operators/windowing/EvictingWindowOperator.java\n\n**Location**: Lines 123-226 (merging windows processElement path)\n\n**Change**: Same as above - add exception handling for the merge process\n\n## Test Case\n\nCreate a test that:\n1.
